title: The Fifth Sunday in Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: March 21, 2021 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Fifth Sunday in Lent, the Sunday before Passion Sunday (Palm Sunday would fall on March 28, 2021). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would likely include Jeremiah 31:31-34 (the new covenant promise), Psalm 51, Hebrews 5:5-10, and John 12:20-33 (the Greeks coming to Jesus and the grain of wheat falling to the ground). The church is in the final, most solemn stretch of Lenten preparation before Holy Week, a time traditionally focused on repentance, self-examination, and meditation on Christ's passion.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the midst of implementing pandemic-related adaptations to worship that had begun in 2020, with many dioceses still navigating return-to-in-person protocols. The Diocese of Colorado was operating under Bishop Kimberly Lucas's leadership (she became diocesan bishop in 2018) as congregations continued managing COVID-19 safety measures. No major General Convention actions or bishop elections were imminent, though the church nationally was still processing the effects of the 2018 General Convention's decisions on marriage and identity.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing early spring weather patterns typical for late March, with the city preparing for the Easter season and spring reopening of various public spaces. The state legislature was in its final weeks of the 2021 session (which ended May 10), with various bills under consideration. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, where Saint John's Cathedral is located on Clarkson Street, was continuing its gradual recovery and revitalization, though local small businesses were still navigating pandemic-related closures and capacity restrictions.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The top national stories included the ongoing COVID-19 vaccination campaign with the U.S. having administered over 100 million doses by this week, President Biden's administration settling into its first 60 days with focus on stimulus spending and infrastructure, and mass shooting at a grocery store in Boulder, Colorado on March 22, 2021 (the day after this sermon), which killed 10 people and became national headlines within 24 hours. Additionally, the trial of Derek Chauvin in the George Floyd killing was ongoing in Minnesota, creating national tension around policing and justice. March Madness college basketball tournament was in full swing.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The global COVID-19 pandemic continued to dominate international news, with vaccine rollouts proceeding unevenly across Europe and the Global South. India was entering a massive second wave of COVID-19 infections that would peak in April-May 2021. The Myanmar military coup that occurred in early February was ongoing, with civil disobedience protests and international condemnation continuing.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was one of cautious optimism mixed with anxiety: vaccination progress offered hope for pandemic recovery, but COVID fatigue was high, and Americans were confronting ongoing racial justice questions in the wake of the Chauvin trial. There was a sense of emerging from a dark period but with significant unresolved tensions. Culturally, streaming entertainment dominated as people were still largely avoiding theaters; the 2021 Academy Awards ceremony had taken place on April 25, 2021 (after this sermon). The cultural conversation was split between pandemic recovery narratives and continued racial reckoning. For Denver specifically, the city had begun its spring season of outdoor activities and cultural reopenings.
